did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an improved EfficientNet deep learning model incorporating Dynamic Convolution (Do-Conv) and Coordinate Attention (CA) for the automated classification of skin cancer types. The study was conducted on the ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 dermatological datasets to achieve classification results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of an improved EfficientNet model (incorporating Do-Conv and Coordinate Attention) for skin cancer classification. It includes a methodology section, experimental setup on ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 datasets, ablation studies, and comparative results, which are characteristic of a research article rather than a review.

title: Diagnosis and Classification of Skin Cancer Types Based on Improved EfficientNet Network
journal: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Pattern Recognition, Machine Vision and Artificial Intelligence (PRMVAI)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of automated skin cancer classification by proposing an improved EfficientNet model that integrates Dynamic Convolution (Do-Conv) and Coordinate Attention (CA) mechanisms. The enhanced architecture aims to capture subtle lesion features and spatial dependencies more effectively than standard convolutional layers. The model was evaluated on the ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 dermatological datasets using an 80:20 train-validation split. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed EfficientNet-DCA achieved classification accuracies of 81.49% on ISIC 2019 and 84.54% on HAM10000. These results represent significant improvements over the baseline EfficientNet and outperform mainstream models such as MobileNet and ResNet. The findings confirm that the combination of Do-Conv and CA enhances feature extraction and robustness for diagnosing various skin cancer types.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin cancer types using the ISIC 2019 (8 classes: NV, BCC, AK, BKL, DF, VASC, SCC, MEL) and HAM10000 (7 classes: NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC, MEL) datasets. The model predicts discrete lesion categories, achieving classification accuracies of 81.49% on ISIC 2019 and 84.54% on HAM10000.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset provided by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) containing standardized dermoscopic imagery for automated detection of malignant melanoma and related cutaneous abnormalities.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the individual and combined contributions of the Do-Conv module and Coordinate Attention (CA) mechanism on classification accuracy and loss.
To compare the performance of the proposed EfficientNet-DCA model against baseline EfficientNet and other mainstream models (MobileNet, ResNet) using accuracy, loss, AUC, and confusion matrices.
To assess classification performance, including correct identifications and misclassifications for specific lesion types.
To evaluate the discriminative ability of the models, specifically reporting micro-average and macro-average AUC values.
To partition the ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 datasets for model training and validation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on classification accuracy, loss, and AUC metrics without statistical significance testing.

ablation_study.notes: The EfficientNet-DCA model achieved the maximum accuracy of 81.49% with a loss of 0.0130. This represents an improvement of 3.36% over the original EfficientNet model.

comparative_experiments.notes: EfficientNet-DCA outperformed all listed baseline models. Comparative ROC analysis showed improved micro-average AUC (0.97→0.98) and macro-average AUC (0.95→0.97) for EfficientNet-DCA over baseline EfficientNet. Correct NV case identifications rose from 2,244 to 2,383 instances.

confusion_matrix_details.notes: Correct NV case identifications increased from 2,244 to 2,383 instances for the EfficientNet-DCA model compared to the baseline.

ablation_study.notes: The EfficientNet-DCA model achieved an accuracy of 84.54% with a loss of 0.0089. This represents an improvement of 2.43% over the original EfficientNet model.

comparative_experiments.notes: EfficientNet-DCA outperformed all listed baseline models.

roc_curve_analysis.notes: The EfficientNet-DCA model showed improved ROC performance with micro-average AUC increasing from 0.97 to 0.98. Macro-average AUC remained at 0.96. Notable improvements were seen in akiec, df, and bkl categories.

modifications: Replaced Mobile Inverted Bottleneck Convolution (MBConv) with Depthwise Over-parameterized Convolution (Do-Conv)
Incorporated Coordinate Attention (CA) mechanism post-convolutional operations
architecture_details.do_conv_details: Over-parameterized approach decomposing convolution into independently learnable steps (depthwise convolution and linear transformation/matrix multiplication) fused during inference
architecture_details.ca_details: Bidirectional feature aggregation (horizontal and vertical 1D average pooling) to capture spatial dependencies and channel-wise attention
architecture_details.layers: Hierarchical feature extraction via Do-Conv layers, spatial saliency modulation via attention weighting, predictive classification via dense neural connections

training_details.preprocessing: Geometric transformations (rotation, flipping) and synthetic sample generation

classification_or_output_head.output_classes: 8 classes for ISIC 2019 (NV, BCC, AK, BKL, DF, VASC, SCC, MEL); 7 classes for HAM10000 (NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC, MEL)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation and flipping were applied to the input images.
Synthetic samples were generated to mitigate class imbalance in the datasets.

conclusions: The proposed EfficientNet-DCA model, which integrates Dynamic Convolution (Do-Conv) and Coordinate Attention (CA), achieves classification accuracies of 81.49% on the ISIC 2019 dataset and 84.54% on the HAM10000 dataset.
The integration of Do-Conv and CA mechanisms results in performance improvements of 3.36% and 2.43% over the baseline EfficientNet model on the ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 datasets, respectively.
The EfficientNet-DCA architecture outperforms mainstream models such as MobileNet and ResNet in comparative experiments on both benchmark datasets.

Ablation studies confirm that the Do-Conv module enhances local feature extraction while the CA mechanism captures spatial contextual dependencies, with their combination providing complementary benefits for feature extraction and attention optimization.

The model demonstrates improved discriminative ability, evidenced by increased micro-average AUC (from 0.97 to 0.98 on ISIC 2019 and HAM10000) and macro-average AUC (from 0.95 to 0.97 on ISIC 2019), as well as significant AUC improvements in challenging or minority classes such as BKL, MEL, DF, and VASC.
The proposed framework effectively addresses data imbalance issues, showing notable improvements in classification performance for small-sample categories within imbalanced scenarios.

The study provides a robust framework for automated skin cancer detection, enhancing diagnostic efficiency and accuracy, with potential for future research in multi-modal data fusion and clinical optimization.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on retrospective dermoscopic datasets (ISIC 2019 and HAM10000) with an 80:20 train-validation split; there is no independent external test set or clinical deployment validation, limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings.

Both datasets exhibit severe class imbalance (e.g., DF is 0.94% in ISIC 2019 and 1.15% in HAM10000; VASC is 1.00% and 1.42% respectively), which may bias performance metrics despite the use of synthetic sample generation for mitigation.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the reported performance improvements over baseline models.
The model was trained on a single hardware configuration (RTX 4090 GPU) using PyTorch 1.6; computational efficiency and inference latency, critical for clinical deployment, are not reported.

Performance on rare classes such as Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular Lesions (VASC) remains limited by their low sample counts, potentially affecting the reliability of the model for these specific diagnoses in diverse populations.

The study addresses intra-class variation and inter-class similarity but does not evaluate robustness to confounding variables such as uneven lighting, motion blur, or different dermoscope brands, which are common in clinical practice.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized two dermatological datasets, ISIC 2019 and HAM10000, for model development and evaluation. Each dataset was independently partitioned using an 80:20 split for training and validation. No separate testing set or external validation cohort was used; the validation subset served as the internal test set. The models were trained on the 80% portion and evaluated on the 20% portion of each respective dataset. Synthetic sample generation and geometric transformations were used for data augmentation to address class imbalance.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper did not perform cross-dataset validation. The model was trained and tested on the same dataset (ISIC 2019 or HAM10000) using an internal 80:20 split. There was no experiment where the model was trained on one dataset and tested on a different, unseen dataset.

training.notes: Used for model fitting via 80:20 split. Preprocessed with geometric transformations and synthetic sample generation.
Used for model fitting via 80:20 split. Preprocessed with geometric transformations and synthetic sample generation.

validation.notes: Used for model selection/tuning and internal performance evaluation. The paper refers to this as the 'validation subset' but uses it for final reporting of accuracy and loss.
Used for model selection/tuning and internal performance evaluation. The paper refers to this as the 'validation subset' but uses it for final reporting of accuracy and loss.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: The baseline EfficientNet-B0 model typically uses ImageNet pretraining, though the paper does not explicitly state the pretraining source for the baseline or the proposed model. The focus is on the architectural modifications (Do-Conv and CA).

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Synthetic sample generation was implemented to mitigate severe class imbalance. Geometric transformations (rotation, flipping) were also applied.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 80:20 ratio for training and validation subsets respectively for both ISIC 2019 and HAM10000 datasets.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The study relies exclusively on the internal 20% validation split of the training datasets for performance reporting.

dataset_level_results.notes: Results from the 20% validation split. EfficientNet-DCA outperformed baseline EfficientNet (78.13% accuracy). Comparative experiments also reported against AlexNet, ShuffleNetV3, MobileNetV2, and ResNet34.

Results from the 20% validation split. EfficientNet-DCA outperformed baseline EfficientNet (82.11% accuracy). Comparative experiments also reported against AlexNet, ShuffleNetV3, MobileNetV2, and ResNet34.

generalizability_notes: The study relies exclusively on retrospective dermoscopic datasets (ISIC 2019 and HAM10000) with an 80:20 train-validation split.
There is no independent external test set or clinical deployment validation, limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings.

Both datasets exhibit severe class imbalance (e.g., DF is 0.94% in ISIC 2019 and 1.15% in HAM10000; VASC is 1.00% and 1.42% respectively), which may bias performance metrics despite the use of synthetic sample generation for mitigation.
The model was trained on a single hardware configuration (RTX 4090 GPU) using PyTorch 1.6; computational efficiency and inference latency, critical for clinical deployment, are not reported.

Performance on rare classes such as Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular Lesions (VASC) remains limited by their low sample counts, potentially affecting the reliability of the model for these specific diagnoses in diverse populations.

The study addresses intra-class variation and inter-class similarity but does not evaluate robustness to confounding variables such as uneven lighting, motion blur, or different dermoscope brands, which are common in clinical practice.
information_not_reported: No external test set was used.
No patient-level separation details were provided (image-level split assumed).
No formal statistical significance testing (p-values or confidence intervals) was reported.
Pretraining source for the baseline EfficientNet was not explicitly stated (though ImageNet is standard).
Specific counts for the 80:20 split were not explicitly calculated in the text, but derived from total counts (25,331 and 10,015).
